burial.plot_notes: Cheney Cemetery, Manchester CT — buried alongside his wife Adeline Cheney Olcott in the Cheney family burial ground.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited 'The Ranch' from his turfgrass father
narrative_blocks.body: When James B. Olcott retired from active farm management in 1890 he turned over The Ranch — the Manchester family farm with its acre of 1,500 turfgrass specimens — to Walter as eldest surviving son. Walter married into the Cheney silk-manufacturing family of Manchester through his wife Adeline Louise Cheney; he is buried in the Cheney family cemetery alongside her.

cross_connections.display_label: Named after his great-granduncle who died young

naming_inspiration.description: Carries forward the name of his grandfather's brother Walter Hooker Olcott (1802-1832)— the youngest of Rev. Allen Olcott and Cynthia Hooker Olcott's children, who died at age thirty without marrying. Walter the second was born thirty-five years after his namesake's death.

burial.plot_notes: Cheney Cemetery, Manchester CT — Cheney family burial ground.

parents.father_research_notes: Father MAJ James Woodbridge Cheney (1838-1919), Civil War major and a member of the broader Cheney silk-manufacturing family of Manchester Connecticut. Not built into JSON — outside Hooker descent tree per Jalapeño Rule (parent-of-spouse only).
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Harriet Elizabeth Cheney Cheney (1838-1913); also a Cheney by birth — Adeline's parents were Cheney cousins. Not built.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Civil War major Cheney silk dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: Adeline's father Major James Woodbridge Cheney (1838-1919) had served as a Civil War officer and was part of the Cheney silk-manufacturing family that dominated Manchester Connecticut industry. Her marriage to Walter Hooker Olcott brought together two of Manchester's most prominent farming and manufacturing families — the Olcotts of 'The Ranch' and the Cheneys of the silk mills where Walter's father James B. had overseen landscaping decades earlier.

sources.label: Connecticut State Library — James Bradford Olcott Papers

notable.notable_blurb: Turfgrass pioneer; introduced zoysiagrass to America

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed for the Gold Rush at age eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: In 1849 James B. sailed from Connecticut to San Francisco aboard the Henry Lee as part of the California Gold Rush, age eighteen. He worked for an import company specializing in exotic Pacific plants, ran a tavern in Sacramento, then sailed around the world collecting grass specimens — visiting Hawaii, Korea, Britain, France, and Germany.

narrative_blocks.header: Introduced zoysiagrass to the United States
narrative_blocks.body: From 1889 under the auspices of the Connecticut Agricultural Experiment Station, James B. began an obsessive turfgrass research project at his Manchester farm 'The Ranch.' One acre held more than 1,500 distinct grass specimens in five-square-foot plots, which he mowed and maintained using tools of his own invention. He traveled to Europe, Australia, and New Zealand collecting samples, and was the first to introduce zoysiagrass to America.

narrative_blocks.header: Advised Olmsted on Central Park and Bushnell lawns
narrative_blocks.body: Frederick Law Olmsted consulted James B. Olcott on the lawns of his most famous landscape designs — New York's Central Park and Hartford's Bushnell Park. Olcott's reputation as 'the Grass Man' brought him into the inner circle of the most influential landscape architect of the era.

narrative_blocks.header: Founding trustee of the Storrs Agricultural School
narrative_blocks.body: In 1880 James B. served on a three-person Connecticut General Assembly committee — with William Henry Brewer of Yale and George A. Bowen — that reviewed Charles and Augustus Storrs's offer to donate land and money for a state agricultural school. He was appointed to the school's first board of trustees in 1881 and stepped down in 1888 after advocating Yale retain its land-grant status.

narrative_blocks.header: Worked for Cheney Brothers and the Connecticut Grange
narrative_blocks.body: Between his farm and his turfgrass work, James B. oversaw outdoor work — landscaping and road construction — for the Cheney Brothers silk-manufacturer company town in South Manchester. He co-founded the Manchester chapter of the Connecticut State Grange in 1885; some peers thought him 'something of a crank.'

cross_connections.display_label: Advised Olmsted on Central Park and Bushnell Park lawns

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries his maternal grandmother Alice Bradford's surname forward — the line tracing back to William Bradford, second governor of Plymouth Colony.

education.notes: Harwinton CT. Attended after returning from California.

career.notes: Inherited and managed family farm. Retired 1890; turned over to son Walter.
Wrote agriculture column for two decades.
Oversaw landscaping and road construction in the Cheney Brothers company town.

career.organization: Storrs Agricultural School (later University of Connecticut)
career.notes: Served on the original three-person committee (with William Henry Brewer of Yale and George A. Bowen) that recommended the legislature accept Charles and Augustus Storrs's 1880 land-and-money donation. Subsequently appointed to the school's first board of trustees in 1881; resigned 1888 after advocating Yale retain land-grant status.

institutions.institution_blurb: Founding trustee of the Storrs Agricultural School (later University of Connecticut); served on the original review committee 1880 alongside William Henry Brewer of Yale.

parents.father_research_notes: Father Isaac Roberts of East Hartford CT.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother not preserved in available sources.

burialCemetery.name: East Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Manchester Connecticut municipal cemetery. Burial site of Polly Olcott Belden (HD3416) and her husband Donald L. Belden (I00356); Roger Olcott (HD3417) and both of his wives Janet Jessup Olcott (I00357) and Dorothy Denton Olcott (I00358).
